title: Titus 2
subtitle: Sound Doctrine and the Grace of God

sections.header: Verses 1–10 — "Teach What Accords with Sound Doctrine"

heb.paragraph: The σωφρον- word group (self-control, sound-mindedness) appears in every sub-section of this chapter: older men (v. 2), older women (v. 4, teaching younger women), young men (v. 6), and Titus himself (v. 12). It is the dominant virtue of the Pastoral Epistles, appearing more frequently than any other ethical term. In Greek philosophy, σωφροσύνη was a cardinal virtue denoting rational self-mastery. Paul Christianizes it as Spirit-empowered discipline.

refs.note: The Colossian household code provides the closest structural parallel, addressing wives, husbands, children, fathers, and slaves in sequence.
The Ephesian household code is the most developed, especially the marriage section. Together with Colossians and Titus, these three texts constitute the Pauline household-code tradition.
Peter's household code, addressed to slaves, wives, and husbands, shares the same concern for public witness and social respectability.

notes.note: Teach what accords with sound doctrine. Older men must be temperate, worthy of respect, self-controlled, and sound in faith, love, and endurance.

Older women must be reverent in behavior, not slanderers, not addicted to much wine, teaching what is good. They must train younger women to love their husbands and children, to be self-controlled and pure.
Encourage young men to be self-controlled. In everything set them an example by doing what is good. In your teaching show integrity, seriousness, and soundness of speech.

Slaves must be subject to their masters, trying to please them, not arguing with them, not stealing. Instead, they should show complete trustworthiness, so that they make the teaching about God our Savior attractive.

Sound doctrine must produce sound living. Paul organizes his instruction by social group, but the common thread is self-control and conduct that commends the gospel to outsiders. The older must teach the younger. Slaves must serve with integrity. The church's public witness depends on every member's conduct. Religion without visible transformation is empty profession.
The purpose clause 'so that no one will malign the word of God' reveals the missionary motive behind the household code: domestic conduct is a form of evangelistic witness.
The phrase 'make the teaching attractive' (κοσμῶσιν, lit. 'to adorn/beautify') is a striking metaphor: ethical behavior 'decorates' the gospel, making it visually appealing to outsiders.

The household code is structured by demographic: older men (v. 2), older women/young women (vv. 3–5), young men/Titus (vv. 6–8), slaves (vv. 9–10). The dominant virtue is σωφροσύνη (self-control), which appears in every section. The missiological rationale surfaces in three purpose clauses: that the word not be reviled (v. 5), that opponents be silenced (v. 8), that the teaching be adorned (v. 10). Ethics is evangelism.

The household code adapts the Greco-Roman Haustafel tradition for Christian community formation. Each group receives instructions calibrated to their social role and the specific vulnerabilities of the Cretan context. The three purpose clauses (vv. 5, 8, 10) reveal the code's underlying logic: domestic ethics serves the church's mission by demonstrating the gospel's transformative power in the most visible social structures. The slave instruction (vv. 9–10) is particularly significant: the lowest-status group is charged with the highest honor—'adorning' the teaching of God the Savior.

hist.context: The household code (Haustafel) in vv. 1–10 organizes instructions by social group: older men (v. 2), older women (vv. 3–4a), young women (vv. 4b–5), young men (vv. 6–8), and slaves (vv. 9–10). This pattern parallels Col 3:18–4:1 and Eph 5:22–6:9 but adapts the form to the Cretan context. The consistent refrain is that good conduct makes the gospel attractive to outsiders: 'so that the word of God may not be reviled' (v. 5), 'so that opponents may be put to shame' (v. 8), 'so that they will make the teaching about God our Savior attractive' (v. 10).

sections.header: Verses 11–15 — "The Grace of God Has Appeared"

heb.paragraph: The verb ἐπιφαίνω (epiphaino, to appear, to shine forth) is the key theological term in Titus. Grace itself is personified as appearing—a dramatic image that presents the Christ-event as a cosmic dawn. The term ἐπιφάνεια was used in the Greco-Roman world for divine manifestations and royal visits, making Paul's use simultaneously christological and counter-imperial.

The phrase 'the blessed hope' (τὴν μακαρίαν ἐλπίδα) is closely linked to 'the appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ' (v. 13). The grammatical construction—one article governing both 'hope' and 'appearing'—suggests they are aspects of the same event. The Granville Sharp rule applied to 'our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ' strongly supports reading this as a direct attribution of deity to Christ.

refs.note: Paul's declaration that salvation is by grace through faith, not by works, provides the broader Pauline context for the grace-theology articulated here.
The already/not-yet structure of salvation—justified by faith (past), standing in grace (present), rejoicing in hope of glory (future)—parallels the three-tense structure of Tit 2:11–14.
Peter's parallel construction 'our God and Savior Jesus Christ' (using the same Granville Sharp construction) supports the deity-of-Christ reading in Tit 2:13.

notes.note: The grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation to all people. It teaches us to say 'No' to ungodliness and worldly passions, and to live self-controlled, upright, and godly lives in this present age.
We wait for the blessed hope—the appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ. This is the Christian's ultimate expectation: not death but glory.

Christ gave himself for us to redeem us from all wickedness and to purify for himself a people that are his very own, eager to do what is good. These are the things to teach, encourage, and rebuke with all authority. Do not let anyone despise you.

Grace is not merely forgiveness but a teacher: it instructs us to deny ungodliness and to live soberly, justly, and devoutly. The blessed hope of Christ's appearing sustains us in the present struggle. Christ's self-giving has a double purpose: redemption from wickedness and purification of a people zealous for good works. Grace and works are not opposed but united: grace produces the good works it demands.

The phrase 'our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ' applies the Granville Sharp rule: when two singular substantives are connected by καί and the first has the article, both refer to the same person. This is one of the clearest NT affirmations of Christ's deity.
The phrase 'a people for his own possession' (λαὸν περιούσιον) echoes the LXX of Exod 19:5 and Deut 14:2, applying Israel's covenant identity to the church.

The passage is structured around two ἐπιφάνειαι (appearings): grace appeared in the incarnation (v. 11, past) and glory will appear at the parousia (v. 13, future). Between these two epiphanies, grace 'trains' (παιδεύουσα) believers to live ethically in the present. The Granville Sharp construction in v. 13 almost certainly identifies Jesus as μεγάλου θεοῦ (great God)—the most explicit christological title in the Pastorals.

The redemption statement echoes both Ps 130:8 (‘he will redeem Israel from all their sins’) and Exod 19:5 (‘a treasured possession’). Christ's self-giving accomplishes both liberation (from lawlessness) and formation (a people zealous for good works)—the Pastorals' persistent connection of soteriology and ethics.

This is the letter's theological center of gravity. The two-epiphany structure (past appearance of grace, future appearance of glory) creates an eschatological framework within which the ethical instructions of vv. 1–10 make sense: Christian conduct is not mere social conformity but participation in a salvation-historical drama stretching from incarnation to parousia. The personification of grace as 'appearing' and 'training' is theologically innovative: grace is not merely an attitude of God but a historical event with pedagogical consequences. The ecclesiological formula 'a people for his own possession' transfers covenant identity from Israel to the church—a significant claim in a letter addressed to a Gentile community.

hist.context: Titus 2:11–14 is widely recognized as one of the most important soteriological passages in the Pastorals and possibly in the entire NT. It moves through three temporal frames: past (grace appeared, v. 11), present (training us to renounce ungodliness, v. 12), and future (the blessed hope and appearing of glory, v. 13). The passage functions as the theological foundation for the ethical instructions that precede and follow it: grace is both the source of salvation and the teacher of godly living. The christological title 'our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ' (v. 13) is one of the clearest affirmations of Christ's deity in the Pauline corpus.

themes.note: Mercy and Holiness dominate: grace appears to save and to train in godly living. Mission pervades through the three purpose clauses in the household code. Sovereignty and Faith frame the two-epiphany soteriology.

rows.label: vv.11–15
rows.text: The two epiphanies: grace and glory

lit.note: Chapter 2 moves from practical household instruction (vv. 1–10) to its theological foundation (vv. 11–15). The ethical code and the grace passage are inseparable: grace teaches godly living, and godly living adorns the gospel.

hebtext.note: The σωφρον- word group (self-control, sound-mindedness) appears in every sub-section of this chapter: older men (v. 2), older women (v. 4, teaching younger women), young men (v. 6), and Titus himself (v. 12). It is the dominant virtue of the Pastoral Epistles, appearing more frequently than any other ethical term. In Greek philosophy, σωφροσύνη was a cardinal virtue denoting rational self-mastery. Paul Christianizes it as Spirit-empowered discipline.

The verb ἐπιφαίνω (epiphaino, to appear, to shine forth) is the key theological term in Titus. Grace itself is personified as appearing—a dramatic image that presents the Christ-event as a cosmic dawn. The term ἐπιφάνεια was used in the Greco-Roman world for divine manifestations and royal visits, making Paul's use simultaneously christological and counter-imperial.

The phrase 'the blessed hope' (τὴν μακαρίαν ἐλπίδα) is closely linked to 'the appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ' (v. 13). The grammatical construction—one article governing both 'hope' and 'appearing'—suggests they are aspects of the same event. The Granville Sharp rule applied to 'our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ' strongly supports reading this as a direct attribution of deity to Christ.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Titus
ppl.text: Titus 2:11–14 is widely recognized as one of the most important soteriological passages in the Pastorals and possibly in the entire NT…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Sound Doctrine and the Grace of God

translations.text: Likewise, teach the older women to be reverent in the way they live, not to be slanderers or addicted to much wine, but to teach what is good.
Older women likewise are to be reverent in behavior, not slanderers or slaves to much wine. They are to teach what is good,
to be self-controlled and pure, to be busy at home, to be kind, and to be subject to their husbands, so that no one will malign the word of God.
to be self-controlled, pure, working at home, kind, and submissive to their own husbands, that the word of God may not be reviled.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Teach what accords with sound doctrine. Older men must be temperate, worthy of respect, self-controlled, and sound in faith, love, and endurance."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Sound doctrine must produce sound living. Paul organizes his instruction by social group, but the common thread is self-control and conduct that commends the gospel to outsiders. The older must teach …"

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The purpose clause 'so that no one will malign the word of God' reveals the missionary motive behind the household code: domestic conduct is a form of evangelistic witness."

discourse.title: Titus 2 — Sound Doctrine for Every Group and Grace that Trains
discourse.thesis: Teach what accords with sound doctrine: specific instruction for older men, older women, young women, young men, and slaves. The grace of God trains us for godly living.

nodes.label: Older men (2:1-2)
nodes.text: Teach what accords with sound doctrine. Older men are to be sober-minded, dignified, self-controlled, sound in faith, in love, and in steadfastness.

nodes.label: Older women (2:3-5)
nodes.text: Older women likewise are to be reverent in behavior, not slanderers or slaves to wine. They are to train the young women to love their husbands and children, to be self-controlled, pure, working at home, kind, submissive to their own husbands, so that the word of God may not be reviled.

nodes.label: Young men (2:6-8)
nodes.text: Urge the younger men to be self-controlled. Show yourself a model of good works, with integrity and dignity in teaching, with sound speech that cannot be condemned, so that an opponent may have nothing evil to say about us.

nodes.label: Slaves (2:9-10)
nodes.text: Slaves are to be submissive to their own masters, well-pleasing, not argumentative, not pilfering, but showing all good faith, so that in everything they may adorn the doctrine of God our Savior.

nodes.label: Grace appeared (2:11-14)
nodes.text: For the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation for all people, training us to renounce ungodliness and worldly passions, and to live self-controlled, upright, and godly lives in the present age, waiting for our blessed hope — the appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ, who gave himself to redeem us from all lawlessness and purify for himself a people for his own possession who are zealous for good works.

nodes.label: Declare with authority (2:15)
nodes.text: Declare these things; exhort and rebuke with all authority. Let no one disregard you.

discourse.note: The 'grace appeared' passage (2:11-14) is a theological gem: grace not only saves but trains. The Christology ('our great God and Savior Jesus Christ') is among the clearest in the NT.

coaching.tip: Sound doctrine produces sound living (vv. 1-10). Older men, older women, younger women, younger men, slaves—each category receives practical instruction rooted in theological reality. Belief and behavior interlock; separating them distorts both.

'The grace of God has appeared' (v. 11)—epiphany language for the incarnation. Grace isn't abstract; it 'appeared.' It teaches us to say no to ungodliness (v. 12). Grace disciples; it doesn't merely excuse.
